From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

| Languages of Norway | |
| --- | --- |
| [Official](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") | [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language")[[a]](#cite_note-1)  [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages")[[b]](#cite_note-sami-2) |
| [Minority](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") | [Kven Finnish](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language")  [Romani](/wiki/Scandoromani_language "Scandoromani language")  [Romanes](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language") |
| Foreign | [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") (>90%) |
| [Signed](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") | [Norwegian Sign Language](/wiki/Norwegian_Sign_Language "Norwegian Sign Language") |
| [Keyboard layout](/wiki/Keyboard_layout "Keyboard layout") | Norwegian [QWERTY](/wiki/QWERTY "QWERTY") |

Many languages are spoken, written and [signed](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway").

In Norway, the indigenous languages, [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language") and [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages"),[[b]](#cite_note-sami-2) have official status. Out of them, Norwegian is the most widely spoken language in Norway. [English](/wiki/English_(language) "English (language)"), a foreign language, is the second most widely spoken language in Norway. As of 2013,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit) there are 4.5 million fluent English-speakers (approximately 88% of the Norwegian population).

## Norwegian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Norwegian")]

The most widely spoken language in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") is Norwegian. It is a [North Germanic language](/wiki/North_Germanic_languages "North Germanic languages"), closely related to [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") and [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), all linguistic descendants of [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse"). Norwegian is used by some 95% of the population as a first language. The language has two separate [written standards](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"): [Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk "Nynorsk") ("New Norwegian", "New" in the sense of contemporary or modern as opposed to old Norse) and [Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l "Bokmål") ("Book Language/Tongue/Speech"), both of which are official.

### Norwegian language struggle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Norwegian language struggle")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Denmark-Norway_in_1780.svg/250px-Denmark-Norway_in_1780.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Denmark-Norway_in_1780.svg)

The European portions of the Kingdom of Denmark-Norway until 1814.

Known as *Språkstriden* in Norwegian, the [Norwegian language struggle](/wiki/Norwegian_language_struggle "Norwegian language struggle") is a movement rooted in both Norwegian nationalism and the 400 years of [Danish](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") rule in Norway (*see [Denmark-Norway](/wiki/Denmark-Norway "Denmark-Norway")*). The [koiné language](/wiki/Koin%C3%A9_language "Koiné language") (mixed language) known as [Dano-Norwegian](/wiki/Dano-Norwegian "Dano-Norwegian") (*Dansk-Norsk*) which developed in Norwegian cities was the result of Danish replacing Norwegian as the language of the upper classes in that country (Danish was used in the courts of law and by the ruling class, and after the [Lutheran Reformation](/wiki/Lutheran_Reformation "Lutheran Reformation") of 1536 it replaced [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") as a [liturgical language](/wiki/Liturgical_language "Liturgical language")). The adoption of a few elements of Norwegian orthography into the Danish language gave rise to the written standard of [Riksmål](/wiki/Riksm%C3%A5l "Riksmål"), which later became Bokmål. Nynorsk, a new standard of Norwegian based upon the spoken language in rural Norway, was acknowledged by the parliament in 1885, and in 1892 it was first possible to use Nynorsk as a language of primary instruction. By 1920, Nynorsk was being used widely in western Norway and the mountain valleys, where it still has its stronghold, and Bokmål was used in the more populous areas of the country. Later, attempts were made to reconcile the two standards into *Samnorsk*, or "Common Norwegian", although this never came to fruition.

### Bokmål

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Bokmål")]

Main article: [Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l "Bokmål")

Bokmål, the written language of some 80% of the Norwegian population[[1]](#cite_note-sprakrad-3) and understood by practically the entire population, is based on a combination of Danish and Nynorsk. It differs from its mostly Danish predecessor Riksmål in terms of genders, lexicon, counting system, a tendency to permit concrete noun endings in abstract situations and diphthongs versus single vowels. Riksmål was officially changed to Bokmål in 1929.

### Nynorsk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Nynorsk")]

Main article: [Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk "Nynorsk")

Nynorsk was developed by the linguist [Ivar Aasen](/wiki/Ivar_Aasen "Ivar Aasen") in the 1850s, based on rural, spoken Norwegian, rather than the cultured, Danish-influenced Norwegian spoken in cities. Its first official codification was in 1901, was given the name Nynorsk in 1929, and has been used officially (alongside Bokmål) since 1938. Its usage, however has declined: in 1944 it was used by 34.1% (the highest recorded number), in 1971 by 17.5% of the population, today, some 15% of schoolchildren are taught Nynorsk as their written language, and Nynorsk is reportedly used as the main form of Norwegian by around 7.4% of the total population, whereas an additional 5% switch between Bokmål and Nynorsk.[[1]](#cite_note-sprakrad-3)

Nynorsk is most commonly used in the western and southern parts of Norway, and is much less common in areas within range of [European route E6](/wiki/European_route_E6 "European route E6") (Eastern Norway, Trøndelag, and northern Norway). Though Nynorsk is understood by a large majority of the country's population, it is not widely understood among teenagers, and it is frequently discussed whether to continue to make Nynorsk obligatory in schooling or not.[[2]](#cite_note-4)[[3]](#cite_note-5)

Høgnorsk

A more conservative variation of Nynorsk exists, called [Høgnorsk](/wiki/H%C3%B8gnorsk "Høgnorsk"). It has few active users, but is supported by the [Ivar Aasen-sambandet](/wiki/Ivar_Aasen-sambandet "Ivar Aasen-sambandet") organization, founded in 1965 in response to the [samnorsk](/wiki/Samnorsk "Samnorsk") policy of the government at the time.

## Sámi languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Sámi languages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Sami_languages_large.png/250px-Sami_languages_large.png)](/wiki/File:Sami_languages_large.png)

Traditional geographic distribution of the Sami languages: 1. South Sami, 4. Lule Sami, 5. Northern Sami

The [Sami people](/wiki/Sami_people "Sami people") are [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_people "Indigenous people") to Northern Scandinavia,[[4]](#cite_note-6)[[5]](#cite_note-7) and though they have [largely adopted](/wiki/Language_shift "Language shift") Norwegian, Swedish, Finnish, or [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language")—due in no small part to official assimilation policies[[6]](#cite_note-8)—some still speak their [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_languages "Indigenous languages") [Sámi languages](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages"). Alongside speaking those languages, practically all speakers of Sámi languages in Norway also have Bokmål as their second or first language.

Sámi languages, like Kven and Finnish, belong to the [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") language family. By far the most spoken form of Sami in Norway is [Northern Sámi](/wiki/Northern_S%C3%A1mi "Northern Sámi"), spoken by around 15,000 Sami in Norway, equivalent to approximately 0.27% of the population. The others are [Lule Sámi](/wiki/Lule_S%C3%A1mi "Lule Sámi") (spoken by around 500 in Norway; 0.01%) and [South Sámi](/wiki/South_S%C3%A1mi "South Sámi") (which has around 300 speakers in Norway). Sámi and Norwegian are the official languages of Norway, and Sámi is protected by the constitution.[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11)

## Kven

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kven")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Norway_Counties_Finnmark_Position.svg/250px-Norway_Counties_Finnmark_Position.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Norway_Counties_Finnmark_Position.svg)

The county of Finnmark

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Norway_Counties_Troms_Position.svg/250px-Norway_Counties_Troms_Position.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Norway_Counties_Troms_Position.svg)

The county of Troms

Spoken by the [Kven people](/wiki/Kven_people "Kven people"), the [Kven language](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language") is a Finnic language, closely related to [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"), and spoken by between 5,000 and 8,000 people (equivalent of up to 0.15% of the population) in northeastern Norway, particularly in [Tromsø](/wiki/Troms%C3%B8_Municipality "Tromsø Municipality") (in [Troms](/wiki/Troms "Troms") county) and also in [Finnmark](/wiki/Finnmark "Finnmark") county. Mirroring the situation of [Meänkieli](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli "Meänkieli") in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), Kven is sometimes considered to be a dialect of Finnish, and has a large degree of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with the language.

## Romani

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Romani")]

The [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") are a traditionally travelling people with roots/heritage from India, and today are spread across all of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").

The [Romani language](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"), an [Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"), [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language") (related to other languages spoken in India today), is split into a great number of dialects. Two of these, [Tavringer Romani](/wiki/Tavringer_Romani "Tavringer Romani") and [Vlax Romani](/wiki/Vlax_Romani "Vlax Romani"), are spoken in Norway, by populations of 6,000 and 500, respectively.

[Scandoromani](/wiki/Scandoromani "Scandoromani") is another Romani dialect indigenous to Norway, as well as Sweden. It is spoken by Romanisæl (Tater) Travellers. Because of the wandering nature of the Romani people, there is no geographic stronghold of the Romani language in Norway.

## Norwegian Traveller (Rodi)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Norwegian Traveller (Rodi)")]

Spoken by the [Indigenous Norwegian Travellers](/wiki/Indigenous_Norwegian_Travellers "Indigenous Norwegian Travellers"), a traditionally Itinerant population who almost exclusively inhabit Southwestern and Southern Norway which have a mixture from Romanisæl, also known as Tater (Norwegian & Swedish Romani) and [Yeniche](/wiki/Yeniche_people "Yeniche people") (German Traveller) populations.

The Norwegian Traveller language, also known as Rodi, is based on Norwegian, but has heavy lexicon borrowing from Romani and German [Rotwelsch](/wiki/Rotwelsch "Rotwelsch"). Rotwelsch lexicon has entered through the Yeniche, and Romani lexicon has entered both from the Scandoromani spoken by the Romanisæl (Tater) Travellers of Norway and the Sinti-Romani dialect, as German Rotwelsch has Sinti influences.

Despite the lexicon of Romani and German Rotwelsch origin, the syntax, grammar and morphology of Rodi is entirely Nordic. Despite intermarriages and admixture from Yeniche Travellers and Romanisæl Travellers, Indigenous Norwegian Travellers have retained their own distinct culture, history, traditions, identity and history.

There is no estimate on how many Norwegian Traveller speakers there are in Norway, but it is known that the language is alive.

## Foreign languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Foreign languages")]

In 2017, [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway")'s **immigrant population** consisted of 883,751 people, making up 16.8% of the country's total population[[10]](#cite_note-ssb-12) (this includes both foreign-born and Norwegian-born with two foreign-born parents, and four foreign-born grandparents). Of this number, 724,987 are foreign-born, while 158,764 are Norwegian-born with foreign-born parents.[[11]](#cite_note-13) The ten most common countries of origin of immigrants residing in Norway are [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") (97,197), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") (37,638), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") (36,315), [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia") (28,696), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") (24,601), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") (22,493), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") (20,823), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") (20,537), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") (19,973) and [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea") (19,957).[[12]](#cite_note-Popt-14) The immigrant population comprises people from a total of 221 countries and autonomous regions,[[13]](#cite_note-:0-15) but 25% of the immigrants are from one of four migrant groups: [Polish](/wiki/Poles_in_Norway "Poles in Norway"), [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians"), [Swedes](/wiki/Swedes "Swedes") and [Somalis](/wiki/Somalis_in_Norway "Somalis in Norway").[[14]](#cite_note-16)

Residents of Norway by non-native [first-language](/wiki/First-language "First-language") (2012, estimation)[[15]](#cite_note-17)

| Language | No. of speakers |
| --- | --- |
| [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian_language "Serbo-Croatian language") | 12,250 |
| [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") | 11,489 |

| [Somali](/wiki/Somali_language "Somali language") | 10,904 |

| [Tigrinya](/wiki/Tigrinya_language "Tigrinya language") | 5,552 |

| [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") | 4,929 |

### English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: English")]

In 2013, there were approximately 4.5 million people in Norway who were capable of conversing in English to some extent, out of a population of 5.1 million.[[16]](#cite_note-18) The country currently scores in the top 5 of the [EF English Proficiency Index](/wiki/EF_English_Proficiency_Index "EF English Proficiency Index") along with its [Nordic neighbours](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries").[[17]](#cite_note-19)

### Other foreign languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Languages_of_Norway&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other foreign languages")]

While pupils can pick [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), or [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") from Year 8 onwards, which becomes mandatory from Year 11 onwards, fluency in those languages is very low in Norway.[[18]](#cite_note-20)

[Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"), being significantly [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutually_intelligible "Mutually intelligible"), is well understood, and speakers of Norwegian and Swedish will usually converse verbally in their languages or in various versions of [Svorsk](/wiki/Svorsk "Svorsk") instead of conversing in English. In regards to school classes and credits, Swedish is not considered a true foreign language, as there are no class subjects specifically for Swedish, and thus no exams or [university](/wiki/University "University") credits dedicated to it either; some Swedish is taught as part of regular Norwegian classes from circa Year 7 onwards.[[19]](#cite_note-21)
